key_insight: Modularity only helps continual learning when the network is in a lower-dimensional, more structured representational regime; in a high-dimensional regime, modular and monolithic models perform similarly.
engineering_relevance: For sequential engineering systems, the benefit of explicit subsystem partitioning depends on how representations are initialized and constrained, not just on having modules at all.
method: Compared a task-partitioned recurrent network against a single-network baseline.
Studied a sequential A-B-A continual learning setup with task similarity varied across tasks.
Induced high- and low-dimensional regimes by manipulating weight scale at initialization.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that in the high-dimensional "lazy" regime both architectures behaved similarly, while in the lower-dimensional "rich" regime the modular network showed graded task-specific subspaces and better compositional organization.
watch_item: The conclusions appear tied to a specific sequential A-B-A setup and initialization-scale-controlled regimes, so the effect may not transfer directly to other tasks or architectures without revalidation.
paper_title: Dimensionality Controls When Modularity Helps in Continual Learning
